name: MoE Decode Optimization (Warp Decode)
trigger: MoE decode, mixture of experts inference, expert routing decode, autoregressive, token generation, batch_size_1, small batch, decode throughput, warp decode, warp_decode
approach: For small-batch MoE decode, flip the parallelism axis from experts to outputs. Traditional MoE decode has 8 stages (5 are bookkeeping). Warp decode compresses to 3: Route → Warp compute → Write. Each warp owns one output neuron, streams weight rows for all top-k experts, accumulates in private FP32 registers, reduces via __shfl_xor_sync butterfly (no SMEM, no barriers, no buffers). Two fused kernels: (1) moe_gate_up_3d_batched — each warp loads gate+up weight rows, streams input activation (read once, reused for both projections), warp-level reduction, applies SiLU(gate)*up, writes one intermediate value. (2) moe_down_3d_batched — each warp loops over all top-k routed experts, loads down-projection weight rows, folds routing weight into running FP32 accumulator, final __shfl_xor_sync butterfly reduction. Achieves 1.84x decode throughput and 1.4x closer to FP32 ground truth (by eliminating intermediate MXFP8 quantization).

evidence.strategy: Explore Level 3 parallelism axis adjustment, optimize fundamental decomposition strategy.
Redesign parallel execution axis to eliminate synchronization overhead and buffer redundancies.
explore persistent kernel design eliminating buffers with direct SM mapping and fused operations.

pitfalls: Warp decode is ONLY for small-batch decode — prefill and large-batch inference still benefit from expert-centric grouped GEMM
The parallelism axis flip (experts → outputs) eliminates 5 of 8 traditional stages (gather, pad, quantize, scatter, reduce) but requires complete kernel rewrite
Each warp must be fully independent — no shared mutable state between warps, all accumulation in private registers
__shfl_xor_sync compiles to PTX shfl.sync.bfly instruction which bypasses shared memory entirely — no L1 round-trips, no bank conflicts, no explicit barriers
Warp decode sustains 3.95 TB/s of 6.8 TB/s peak (58%) on B200 — the gap is from random access patterns in expert routing (non-contiguous expert IDs like 5, 8, 14, 19)
Activations stay in BF16 and accumulators in FP32 throughout — do NOT quantize activations to MXFP8 for decode (this is what gives the 1.4x accuracy improvement)
Input activation vector is read once and reused for both gate and up projections — fusing these two projections is critical for bandwidth efficiency
Scales linearly: doubling output dimension doubles independent warps with no added synchronization
